title: SEGMENT 6 — RRBS — reduced-representation bisulfite sequencing for the rest of us

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — RRBS — reduced-representation bisulfite sequencing for the rest of us

If WGBS is the comprehensive, expensive method, RRBS — reduced-representation bisulfite sequencing — is the budget-friendly compromise that captures most of the biologically interesting methylation at a fraction of the cost.

RRBS was developed by Alex Meissner and colleagues, originally in the lab of Rudolf Jaenisch at the Whitehead Institute, in a 2005 paper, and it became a major workhorse of methylation profiling through the 2010s, particularly for cell-line studies and large epigenetic screens.
The insight behind RRBS is this.
WGBS sequences every cytosine in the genome — including the vast majority of cytosines that sit in CpG-poor, methylation-uninformative regions where nothing biologically interesting is happening.

The most biologically interesting methylation is concentrated in CpG-rich regions — CpG islands at promoters, CpG island shores, and some CpG-rich enhancers — which together make up only a tiny percentage of the genome.
If you could enrich your library specifically for CpG-rich regions before sequencing, you'd capture most of the regulatorily meaningful methylation while sequencing far less total DNA.
That's RRBS.
The mechanism is beautifully simple.
You digest your genomic DNA with the restriction enzyme MspI, which recognizes and cuts the sequence CCGG — a sequence that, by definition, contains a CpG.
MspI is methylation-insensitive, meaning it cuts the same whether the CpG is methylated or not, so it doesn't bias your coverage by methylation state.
After MspI digestion, you get a population of fragments whose ends always abut a CpG site.

The crucial property of MspI digestion is that, because CCGG sites are enriched in CpG-rich regions of the genome and depleted in the CpG-poor bulk, the fragments produced by MspI are biased in size and abundance toward CpG-rich regions.
You then size-select — typically picking fragments in the range of 40 to 220 base pairs — and proceed with bisulfite conversion, library prep, and Illumina sequencing.

The size selection enriches even further for CpG-rich regions, because MspI produces tightly clustered cuts in CpG-dense areas, generating fragments of the right size from those areas while producing very long, excluded fragments from CpG-poor regions.

The result, after sequencing and analysis, is a methylation map that covers somewhere between 1 and 3 million CpGs — roughly 5 to 10 percent of all CpGs in the genome, concentrated in the regulatorily interesting CpG-rich regions, particularly CpG island promoters.

You miss the methylation status of the vast majority of the genome's CpGs, but you capture most of the ones that anyone cared about, at a sequencing cost roughly an order of magnitude lower than WGBS.
Here is the analogy.
RRBS is methylation-on-a-budget.
Instead of sequencing every cytosine in the genome, you use a restriction enzyme to pull out just the CpG-rich fragments, which are the regions where the regulatorily interesting methylation lives.
You get most of the biologically meaningful signal at maybe a tenth of the cost of WGBS.
It's the eighty-twenty rule applied to methylation profiling.

RRBS dominated cell-line methylation studies through the 2010s, particularly in the ENCODE and Roadmap Epigenomics projects, which generated RRBS profiles of dozens of human cell lines and primary tissues.
It's still in active use today, particularly in academic labs that need methylation profiles on many samples and don't have the budget for WGBS.

The trade-offs are well-understood: you don't see methylation in CpG-poor regions, you don't see methylation in enhancers that aren't CpG-rich, and you don't see the so-called partially methylated domains — large stretches of intermediate methylation that turn out to be biologically meaningful in many contexts.
RRBS sees what it's designed to see, and misses the rest.

For most regulatory questions — is this promoter methylated, is this CpG island differentially methylated between conditions, what's the methylation status of this tumor suppressor — RRBS gives you the answer.
For comprehensive epigenomic mapping, including methylation outside CpG islands, you still need WGBS or one of the newer comprehensive methods.
The tool fits the question.

There's also a class of intermediate methods — bisulfite-treated capture sequencing, where you use a hybridization capture step (like exome capture) to enrich for specific regions of interest before sequencing.
This lets you design custom methylation panels — for example, a panel of all the methylation hotspots known to be relevant to cancer diagnosis — and sequence them deeply at low cost per sample.
Capture-based methylation sequencing is common in clinical research, and it's particularly popular for liquid biopsy applications.
It's another point on the cost-comprehensiveness trade-off, sitting between RRBS and WGBS in coverage and customizability.
